---
title: Eriksen Flanker Experiment 
subtitle: Results from The Eriksen Flanker Task Conducted on Psych390 Students 
author:
  - name: Rubee Kung 
    email: rkung@uwaterloo.ca
    affiliations: [University of Waterloo]
    attributes:
        corresponding: true
  - name: Nicole Hua
    email: nhua@uwaterloo.ca
    affiliations: [University of Waterloo]
  - name: Jacob Grima
    email: jgrima@uwaterloo.ca
    affiliations: [University of Waterloo]
  - name: Eda Kubra Ozkaya
    email: ekozkaya@uwaterloo.ca
    affiliations: [University of Waterloo]
affiliations: 
  - id: uw 
    name: University of Waterloo 
    Department: Department of Psychology 
    address: University Ave, W 
    city: Waterloo
    state: Ontario 
    postal-code: N2l3G1

abstract: The Eriksen flanker task is a widely used measure of selective attention and inhibitory control, in which individuals respond to a central target while ignoring surrounding distractor stimuli. The present study aimed to replicate the flanker interference effect using an arrow-based variation. A sample of 16 undergraduate students from the University of Waterloo participated in a within-subjects experiment in which reaction time and accuracy were measured across 20 trials varying in congruency (congruent vs. incongruent) and direction (left vs. right). Results indicated that participants responded significantly slower on incongruent trials compared to congruent trials, F(1, 15) = 56.70, p < .001, and also showed reduced accuracy in incongruent conditions, demonstrating the expected interference effect. No significant effects of direction or interaction between direction and congruency were observed, suggesting that performance differences were driven specifically by distractor congruency. These findings support theories of selective attention and cognitive control by showing that task-irrelevant stimuli are processed automatically and can interfere with response selection. Overall, the results replicate the flanker effect and highlight the utility of the flanker paradigm for examining attentional control.
<<<<<<< HEAD

keywords: 
  - Eriksen flanker task
  - Selective attention
  - Response inhibition
  - Cognitive control
  - Congruency effect
  - Reaction time
  - Repeated measures ANOVA

=======


keywords: 
  - Eriksen Flanker 
  - Congruent 
  - Incongruent 
  - Congruency Effect
  - Selective Attention
  - Response Inhibition 
  - Cognitive Control 
  - Reaction Time 
  - Repeated Measures ANOVA
>>>>>>> ef7a763 (abstract)
date: 2026-4-22
bibliography: bibliography.bib
format:
  elsevier-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    journal:
      name: Journal Name
      formatting: preprint
      # model: 3p # Don't set a model with preprint
      cite-style: authoryear
---

# Intro



# Methods 

used @hkirsch94_github and @marsja_flankercode to build this code. 

# Results 


one in-line stat
one significant statistical analysis 
one plot generated from code -- with appropriate automatic label 
use a repeated meausres anova comparing reaction time as a function of the stimulus.

```{python}
#code built by aider model StepFun: Step 3.5 Flash (free) and edited by ChatGPT
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.stats.anova import AnovaRM

# Read in the CSV data
data = pd.read_csv("csvforanova.csv")

# Filter out rows with missing rt values
data = data.dropna(subset=["rt"])

# Convert rt to numeric
data["rt"] = pd.to_numeric(data["rt"], errors="coerce")
data = data.dropna(subset=["rt"])

# Keep only correct trials if accuracy exists
if "accuracy" in data.columns:
    data = data[data["accuracy"] == 1]

# Keep only main trials if block exists
if "block" in data.columns:
    data = data[data["block"] == "main"]

# Repeated measures ANOVA
# For the standard flanker analysis, use 2 conditions:
# congruent vs incongruent
rm_anova = AnovaRM(
    data=data,
    depvar="rt",
    subject="participant",
    within=["type"],
    aggregate_func="mean"
)

results = rm_anova.fit()

# Print the ANOVA table
print(results)

```

@fig-significantfinding is generated using a python chunk.

```{python}
#| label: fig-significantfinding 
#| fig-cap: A figure of a significant finding of ours using matplotlib.org
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 50%
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd #is not the final plot, will pick a prettier one...
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("csvforanova.csv")
#| print(df.columns)

#must compute participant means 
means = df.groupby(['participant', 'type'])['rt'].mean().reset_index() #computes one mean for each condition (congruent vs incongruent)

#must compute group means 
group_means = means.groupby('type')['rt'].mean()

#we need the standard error of the mean (SEM)
group_sem = means.groupby('type')['rt'].sem()

#values and plotting
conditions = group_means.index
values = group_means.values
errors = group_sem.values
plt.bar(conditions, values, yerr=errors, capsize=5)
plt.ylabel("Reaction Time (ms)")
plt.title("Effect of Congruency on Reaction Time")
plt.show()
```

The template for this plot was found on @matplotlib_website

# Discussion



# Conclusion
Consistent with theories of selective attention and cognitive control, the results of the present study provide clear support for the flanker interference effect [@stoffels1988]. Participants responded significantly slower and less accurately on incongruent trials compared to congruent trials, indicating that task-irrelevant flanker stimuli were automatically processed and interfered with response selection. Importantly, neither the main effect of direction (left vs. right) nor the interaction between direction and congruency was significant, indicating that distractor congruency was the primary driver of performance differences. This then strengthens the interpretation that the observed effects reflect interference from competing stimuli rather than spatial or other response biases. The findings from this study thus help to highlight the limits of cognitive control, as participants were unable to fully suppress distracting information under conditions of response conflict.

These findings support the hypothesis that stimulus incongruency impairs performance by increasing response time and reducing accuracy. The results are consistent with both classic and contemporary research, reinforcing the view that irrelevant stimuli are processed automatically and require additional cognitive control to resolve. This study further highlights the role of selective attention and conflict monitoring in guiding behaviour, as well as the importance of inhibitory processes in managing competing information.

Despite there being a few methodological limitations, including a small and fairly homogeneous sample size, as well as having only a limited number of trials per participant, it is clear that the present study successfully replicates a well-established cognitive effect. Future research could extend these findings by increasing sample size, improving trial balance, and examining individual differences or sequential effects in cognitive control. Overall, the findings demonstrate the robustness of the flanker paradigm as a tool for studying attention and response conflict.

# References {-}
<<<<<<< HEAD
=======

>>>>>>> ef7a763 (abstract)
